game_name: Tom & Jerry: The Ultimate Game of Cat and Mouse!

danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat requiring evasion or combat
Collision damage or stage progression barriers

danger_signals.visual_form: Heart containers in HUD decreasing (whole heart loss per hit)
danger_signals.meaning: Cumulative damage from hits; game over when depleted

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible objects: throwing weapons (hammer, mothballs, jam), swinging weapons (meat cleaver), keys, fuses
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary power-ups or stage progression items

safety_signals.visual_form: Heart item collection; in Japanese version, health restores after sub-levels
Reaching exit points (e.g., key usage at stage 5-2, fuse box at stage 1-1)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Primary enemy type; stage-dependent behavior and visual presentation
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Boss encounters; recurring threat across stages

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Tuffy character (referenced in title screen variant)

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Stage progression (5 stages, multiple sub-levels per stage)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Environmental changes per stage: stage 1 has fuse box mechanics, stage 2 uses meat cleaver, stage 5 features keys; NES console Easter egg at Stage 4-1 (erased in Japanese version)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss difficulty scales; Japanese version bosses require fewer hits; health restoration mechanic differs between Western and Japanese versions

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Difficulty scaling (Western: 3 continues × 3 lives; Japanese: unlimited continues × 5 lives)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: HUD display of remaining lives and continues
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game over conditions vary by region; Japanese version restores health after sub-levels for ease

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stage-specific weapons: hammer (stage 1), plank (stage 2), mothballs (stage 5), invisible ink (stage 1), soap (stage 5); graphic bank conflicts limit visual representation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Weapon effectiveness and animation vary by stage; unused items (jam, egg, fly-swatter, frying pan, bug sprayer, gold key) suggest scrapped mechanics

environmental_storytelling.technique: Stage theming through hazards and items
environmental_storytelling.example: Fuse box in stage 1-1 requires fuse item completion; key usage in stage 5-2 signals final stage progression
NES console background in Stage 4-1 (removed from Japanese release); title variations between regions signal market adaptation
Fractional health loss graphics (medium/small hearts) suggest original damage model; unused food items (jam, egg) and tools indicate cut content or thematic variety

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Sprite approaches and attacks player; stage-dependent AI; boss encounters with varying hit requirements
Full mobility; weapon usage available; can absorb multiple hits
Heart count decreases visually; reduced survival capacity; game over at 0 health
Hazards present; items scattered; boss/stage gates active
Exit accessible; progression enabled; item goals met (e.g., fuse placed, key used)

notes: Tom & Jerry NES uses a simple but effective visual language for a 1991 platformer. Danger is communicated through sprite proximity and environmental hazards; safety through collectibles and health restoration. The game lacks faction complexity—it is purely protagonist vs. antagonist with minor ally presence. Regional differences (Japanese unlimited continues, health restoration, easier bosses) suggest difficulty tuning rather than narrative shifts. Unused graphics (fractional hearts) and items (jam, egg, fly-swatter, frying pan, bug sprayer, gold key, soap) indicate significant cut content, likely due to graphic bank conflicts or development scope reduction. World state is expressed through stage progression, item availability, and environmental changes rather than dynamic faction territory or political shifts.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Tom & Jerry NES article)
